narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and family background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, LSAT score of 30, middle family income bracket, full-time student status, and Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings both at 3—and predicted a 41.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain strong outcomes. Among all the factors considered, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive influence, pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass, since your 3.20 is quite close to the typical student's 3.21 average. Your middle family income bracket was the next most influential, also nudging the model toward a passing prediction, likely reflecting patterns where this socioeconomic level correlates with solid bar performance in the data. However, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3—below the average of about 5.5—emerged as a key counterforce, significantly pushing the prediction toward failure by signaling relatively lower performance compared to peers that year. Your LSAT score of 30, well below the average of 36.7, and Year 1 decile of 3 (versus an average near 5.7) played supporting roles in heightening the risk, though less dominantly. Overall, while your GPA and family background helped balance things out, the dip in later-year school rankings tipped the scales against you relative to typical admitted students who often show stronger decile progress. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male applicant—many in similar demographics succeed with targeted improvements—but the model saw your profile as riskier than the stronger academic trajectories it favors. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your later-year grades to improve decile rankings, and gathering letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience and bar prep potential. You might also explore part-time programs or schools with holistic reviews that weigh personal stories more heavily. With focused effort on these areas, you can reshape your profile for future applications and build the momentum needed to pass the bar confidently.
